seoTitle: Dust & Particulate Control in Indoor Air | CARSI
metaDescription: Control indoor dust and fine particles (PM10, PM2.5, ultrafine) with MERV and HEPA filtration, cleaning and source control. Free AU air quality course.
imageAlt: Technician HEPA-vacuuming carpet while reading a laser particle counter in an Australian office to control dust

og.title: Dust & Particulates in Indoor Air: Control & Cleaning | CARSI
og.description: A free CARSI course on managing indoor airborne dust — from PM10 and PM2.5 to ultrafine particles — through measurement, MERV and HEPA filtration, cleaning, HVAC upkeep and source control.

faq.q: What is the difference between PM10, PM2.5 and ultrafine particles?
faq.a: PM10 are coarse particles 10 micrometres or smaller, such as dust, pollen and mould spores. PM2.5 are fine particles 2.5 micrometres or smaller, often from combustion. Ultrafine particles are below 0.1 micrometres and can reach deep into the lungs and bloodstream.

faq.q: How do MERV and HEPA filters differ?
faq.a: MERV rates a filter's ability to trap particles, from MERV 8 (lint and dust mites) up to MERV 13 (bacteria and smoke). HEPA filters exceed the MERV scale for very fine particles. Higher-rated filters restrict airflow, so the system should be checked before upgrading.

faq.q: What cleaning methods reduce dust without stirring it up?
faq.a: The course recommends damp microfibre cloths over feather dusters, HEPA-equipped vacuums with sealed filtration, mopping after vacuuming, and avoiding dry sweeping — all of which limit the resuspension of settled dust back into the air.

faq.q: Is this course suitable for facility managers?
faq.a: Yes. It is a free course for restoration professionals, facility managers, environmental technicians and anyone responsible for indoor air quality, with no prerequisites.

courseJsonLd.name: Dust and Particulates in Indoor Air: Control and Cleaning Strategies
courseJsonLd.description: A CARSI course on identifying, measuring and controlling indoor airborne dust and particulates using filtration, cleaning, HVAC maintenance and source-control strategies.
